one_line_takeaway: STAC shows that chaining individually benign tool calls jailbreaks tool-enabled LLM agents into harmful real-world actions with >90% success and near-zero detectability, and that current prompt-based defenses only partially and temporarily help.
key_finding: State-of-the-art tool-enabled agents are highly vulnerable to STAC: final ASR exceeds 90% for 7 of 8 agents (GPT-4.1 reaching 93.4% at turn T+2), while attack prompts remain extremely stealthy (PH < 2%) and agents almost never refuse (RR < 4%). The vulnerability is architecture- and scale-independent, rooted in agents' step-by-step tool execution without holistic reasoning over cumulative sequence effects. STAC substantially beats baselines (95.1% ASR vs 72.8% single-turn and 61.5% X-Teaming on n=390). The proposed reasoning-based defense gives the strongest initial protection (cutting GPT-4.1 ASR to 58.6% at turn T, a 28.8-point drop, with RR rising to 29.8%), but its protection erodes sharply against persistent adaptive attackers (ASR rebounds to 86.7% by T+2).
core_contribution: The paper introduces and formalizes Sequential Tool Attack Chaining (STAC), a new class of multi-turn attack against tool-enabled LLM agents in which each tool call appears individually benign but the full ordered sequence collectively produces a harmful, real-world environmental effect that only becomes evident at the final execution step. Its main deliverable is an automated closed-loop pipeline that (a) synthesizes executable multi-step tool chains, (b) validates them via in-environment execution, and (c) reverse-engineers stealthy multi-turn prompts that induce agents to execute the verified malicious final call. The authors also release a benchmark of 483 validated STAC cases (1,352 user-agent-environment interaction sets) and propose a new reasoning-driven (harm-benefit) defense prompt. The paper is careful to distinguish itself from prior multi-turn LLM jailbreaks (which target harmful text generation) and from single-turn agent tool attacks / prompt injection (cited related work, not their contribution).
threat_model: The adversary is a malicious end-user who interacts conversationally with a tool-enabled LLM agent over multiple turns. The attacker cannot access model internals or manipulate tool metadata (distinguishing STAC from prompt injection and from the cited Attractive Metadata Attack); the only lever is crafting a sequence of benign-appearing natural-language requests that each elicit an individually reasonable tool call. Harm is realized not as harmful text but as durable, possibly irreversible environmental changes (e.g., deleting files, misusing banking/workspace tools). The malicious objective is deliberately distributed across turns so that no single prompt or tool output reveals it, defeating per-prompt and per-response safety checks; only full-sequence monitoring can catch it.
limitations: Evaluation is confined to simulated Python environments (SHADE-Arena's 4 environments plus 62 sampled Agent-SafetyBench environments), which may not represent the breadth of real-world agent deployments, tool ecosystems, and security contexts. Only prompt-based defenses are studied (no trained agentic guardrail models or architectural mitigations), owing to a stated lack of effective agentic guardrails. The best defense still leaves ASR >= 58.6% and degrades over turns, so no robust mitigation is demonstrated. The pipeline itself relies on LLM judges (GPT-4.1 as Judge) for scoring ASR/PH/RR, a potential circularity the paper does not deeply audit. Magistral-Small's low ASR (46.2-77.8%) is attributed to capability limits rather than safety, indicating the metric partly conflates inability with resistance.

datasets_used: SHADE-Arena (Kutasov et al., 2025) - all 4 public complex environments: Banking, Travel, Workspace, Spam Filter Updating; extends AgentDojo
Agent-SafetyBench (Zhang et al., 2024) - 62 sampled simpler/diverse environments, source of the 10 agent-specific failure modes
STAC benchmark (this paper's own contribution): 483 validated STAC cases spanning 1,352 user-agent-environment interaction sets
quantitative_results: Final ASR (turn T+2) >90% for 7 of 8 agents; GPT-4.1: 87.4% -> 92.5% -> 93.4% across turns T/T+1/T+2
GPT-4.1-mini highest final ASR: 95.0% at T+2
Magistral-Small lowest ASR: 46.2% / 70.0% / 77.8% (attributed to capability limits, RR <= 3.1%)
Stealth: Prompt Harmfulness (PH) < 2% and Refusal Rate (RR) < 4% across all agents
STAC vs baselines (n=390 SHADE-Arena): STAC ASR 95.1% (2.8 turns) vs Single-turn 72.8% (1.0 turn) vs X-Teaming 61.5% (4.2 turns)
Reasoning defense on GPT-4.1: ASR 58.6% / 80.5% / 86.7% (T/T+1/T+2) vs No Defense 87.4% / 92.5% / 93.4% -- 28.8-point ASR cut at turn T
Reasoning defense RR: 29.8% (T) decaying to 8.1% (T+1) and 5.8% (T+2)
Summarization defense: ASR 79.3% / 84.7% / 87.0%; weaker baselines Spotlighting 85.3% and Failure Modes 85.1% at turn T
baselines_compared: Attack baselines: Single-turn (ablation removing multi-turn context-building) and X-Teaming (Rahman et al., 2025), a SOTA multi-turn LLM jailbreak adapted with tool-call capability
Defense baselines: Spotlighting via Datamarking (Hines et al., 2024, originally anti-prompt-injection) and Failure Modes safety prompt (Zhang et al., 2024)
Authors' own defenses: Summarization prompt and Reasoning (harm-benefit) prompt inspired by SafetyAnalyst (Li et al., 2025)
Target agent models benchmarked: GPT-4.1, GPT-4.1-mini, Qwen3-32B, Llama-3.1-405B-Instruct, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Mistral-Large-Instruct-2411, Mistral-Small-3.2-24B-Instruct, Magistral-Small-2506
